speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four of the interview with Shmuel Huber. It's the 16th of April, 1996. Shmuel, this NKVD officer, we're back with him. He really--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just let me go. Finish, he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish guy?

speaker: interviewee
text: As as a matter of fact-- as a matter of fact-- I don't think he's-- he asked me how many officers like you are here? And when he asked me this question, I realized he is coming from the direction where he has already seen two heroes like myself. So I decided on the spot to tell him the truth. I said, we are four, but we didn't have accommodation. We couldn't get together all four, so I had two of us there and two of us there. He looked at me with such, you know, a mocking look. And to say, I have seen already two like you, two heroes. You are the other two.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he let you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: He let me go. And we arrived [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lviv, or Lemberg, I should call it. Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long were you in Lviv?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Lviv a few days only. One thing that stands out in my mind with Lviv was there was one synagogue left. Lviv had tens of thousands of Jews-- tens of thousands. It was a big city with many Jews. There was one synagogue. And when I arrived, that was, what, five minutes-- five months after the end of the war, the walls of the synagogue were still with blood-- blooded, spots of blood. And we were told by people that they went up with children, throw them down, and said, let your Jewish God catch you.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's how they killed some of the children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just-- what I remember seeing was the blood spots on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the walls of the synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the wall. That was, what is it, five months after the war.

words.text: On the wall. That was, what

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you start to meet any other Jews, any other survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The first survivors I started meeting was I went over to Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Lviv, you went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Lviv. To cross the border at that time was no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get across?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a train or something. I don't remember exact. But I know there was-- there was no-- once I got out this-- this-- train police, there was no problems. As a matter of fact, you see, it was such a flux, everything. People came back from the war, came back from concentration camps, that it was very little check. I arrived in Krakow, which was already over the border from the Soviets.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember Krakow. I found a city completely untouched.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just there. No Jews was there-- a few hundred thousand-- not a few hundred-- wait, wait. The Jewish community was nothing. Just the city existed without the Jews. This is Schindler's city, by the way. I went from Krakow to Lodz. Now, in Lodz, was already a center [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for Jewish refugees. Lodz before the war had a quarter of a million Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find in the way of Jews when you got to Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Nothing. Nothing. Just survivors who came from different places from partisan-- people were in the woods, you know, hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Forests.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz was just big, large rooms. I remember the address. This was where all Jewish people-- so it was all-- it was enough, one house, to house, all the survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: One house?

speaker: interviewee
text: One house. A large house.

speaker: interviewer
text: For everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: For all of us, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the house? Do you remember the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people--

speaker: interviewee
text: To translate it, the-- the Holly Jacob.

speaker: interviewer
text: Holly Jacob Street. 16 Holly Jacob Street.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people do you estimate were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hundreds.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was it? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] These were mostly young men and women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Young men. Less women, of course. I would say that men survive more than women. I would say so. Logically, it is not right, because it's easy to recognize a Jew, a man than a woman, for obvious reasons. But from what I remember, there were more men survivors than women. I think so, at least.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it that there had been this congregation of people in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was organized already. This was organized by Joint.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was Lodz your first--

speaker: interviewee
text: --stop from Soviet Russia. First outside Jewish world after--

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it your first contact with Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was in Lodz that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this house was run by the Joint? It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot say for sure. Joint, Jewish agency, the Bricha. You know what Bricha is, yes? These were the organizations. And besides, there was another thing. The Jews are very political minded, as you may well know. After the whole Holocaust, immediately start popping off the political organizations-- religious one, leftist, rightist. You know, oh from 100 people, 1,000 parties. This was typical of Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was already--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, starting.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was starting already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very beginning. Yes. The very beginning. Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was this group, and that group, and another group--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish life started in a negative way starting-- I don't know if you can see, but it was starting suddenly, you know, like after a fire, you see bits of grass. It's like it came out. This is how I saw it. Suddenly you see political parties that were-- it was anachronism, because it was ridiculous after all of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly. But I remember going from Lodz to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw was disaster, one disaster. Arriving in Warsaw was a shocking experience, because I learned in Warsaw. I spent my childhood in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew it intimately.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew every corner of it, and nothing. Absolutely nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not a Jewish person to be seen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, non-Jewish as well, because there was a second uprising. You have to realize there was a Jewish uprising, and a year later was a Polish uprising. And what the Germans didn't succeed burning the first time they finished off the second time. Actually, all you could see was rubble, pieces of brick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were no people-- Jews, non-Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing, nothing, nothing. Single buildings that were just like a chimney.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a waste land.

speaker: interviewee
text: A waste land. That's the right description. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you walk around there? Did you try to find anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was ridiculous. How can you find-- you-- if somebody will tell you here was the yeshiva, you'd wonder why. It's a brick. Nothing. Nothing was left. Nothing-- no orientation whatsoever. What, where, what? Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't even tell which part of Warsaw vaguely.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. Absolutely. Where I reached out-- reached out, just-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I don't remember exactly if this is what. But in Warsaw, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do you know what Judenrat is? The Judenrat in Warsaw was a certain address. The Judenrat--

speaker: interviewer
text: --was in a building?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a building that survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: That building stayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. Till today. For some reason, I don't know. Maybe the Germans wanted to make a museum to keep Jews alive and to show them through what-- what a Jew looked like when he's alive. I-- I think I went there. I'm not sure. But I think I went there. Anyway, I got a contact in Warsaw. And seeing that I have got nothing to do in Poland, we heard that there was displaced persons camps in Germany. It's a funny thing, but after all this-- after all the concentration of Jews were in Germany after the war. The survivors tended to go. Why, I don't know. The Joint worked-- the Joint worked in Germany. And [INAUDIBLE] near Munich was a large camp. Don't mistake it. It wasn't a concentration. It was a camp for displaced persons. And while I was in-- while I was in Warsaw, I got in contact with some religious Jews. I wasn't religious at all. But they-- I knew their language. I decided I will take them for a ride. So they took me for a ride, and they gave me the contact. And I went over Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left Warsaw. You went through Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: But here, I must stop for one minute. This case that I would like to stress, this is an interesting thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the way somewhere-- I don't know where or when-- I was in a city called Breslau, Wroclaw in Polish. This was Prussia before the war, Germany. While I was walking around there on my way somewhere-- I don't know-- a Polish policeman pulled me up, called me here, took me to the police station, and gave me a good beating. Not for what. Didn't ask me. Didn't talk to me. Didn't accuse me of anything. He didn't have what to accuse me. But while he was beating me up, an officer arrived, and he told him to stop. That was Poland again, I would call it. A chapter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was part of the Polish situation as far as Jews were concerned?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was lucky, because it's well known there was pogroms, and Jews were killed. There was a city called Kielce, and 43 survivors were massacred in one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your beating was a relatively fortunate thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: A beating was relatively fortunate. I have to be grateful. Yes. But this was-- I knew I am in Poland. This was a greeting, a Polish greeting, I would call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's welcome home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Welcome home, yes. And they asked me if I speak Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I went from Warsaw--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Breslau? You got to Breslau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Breslau. I don't remember now, was I in Breslau? No, that was before I reached Warsaw, because from Warsaw I went straight over the border with a group already, organized by the Bricha. And we arrived. We went through Karlsruhe, a well-known place, as I saw in Czechoslovakia. And then we came to Regensburg, and Regensburg to Munich. In Munich was already a big-- a large building where displaced persons were-- all kind-- Jews, non-Jews, all kinds of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, had you established absolutely what had happened to your family by this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely it's impossible to establish. I have got no contact whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had no way of ever finding out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No way of knowing. All I know, just stories from reading what happened here, what happened there. When-- because the made-- the Germans had-- every few months they made [GERMAN] it's called in German. The made-- how shall I describe it-- an event, taking first the poor ones, and the intellectuals, and then every time-- and you know. So somewhere in September of '42 was the last time when Jews were taken out from that place and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: That was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And got to the end. And that's it.

words.text: And got to the end. And that's

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you ended up in this displaced persons camp in Munich. Did it have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Munich was Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the camp didn't have a particular name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was not a camp. This is a large building. And God knows, maybe the Gestapo was there before. A very large building--

speaker: interviewer
text: A big building in Munich itself.

speaker: interviewee
text: A big building, yes. In Munich itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Run by Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not necessarily. There were all kinds of people there from all over the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just simply refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Waiting out. Refugees, yes. Waiting out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not necessarily Jewish refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not necessarily refugees, no. There I was approached by a woman who was a survivor from Warsaw ghetto, from the last, the ones who went out in the-- on the-- on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sewers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Krysza.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was one of the last--

speaker: interviewee
text: Last of the survivors. She was a-- a very fighting woman, yes. Small but very energetic type person.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she say to you? What-- what did she approach you about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember her going around, just looking. Who, where, when-- until she came to me and asked me who I am, what I was, and where I would like to go. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she gave me a choice of two camps. One was Landsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Landsberg is a well-known place. Hitler, as a matter of fact, after his putsch, the Munich Putsch in 1923-- it's a well-known event-- sat there in jail in Landsberg. Anyway, she asked me, what-- would I like to go there at a Jewish camp in Landsberg or Fernwald? And for some reason, I don't know why I said Fernwald, and I met my wife. So you went to Fernwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: And joined a kibbutz. In kibbutz there were so many people-- I told you, the political groupings started. There was the rightists, and as I always was more to the left oriented. So I took the [NON-ENGLISH], I would call it now. You see, not extreme left, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Kind of left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Socialist. Yeah, socialist type.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you met your wife-- your wife to be there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. First week on a Friday evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about meeting her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, this I can remember. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was an event. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know. This story has to be told from my, in my opinion, from two people, not from one. Because you have to realize, when I met her, she met me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's hear your side of the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] In a nutshell, I was very impressed. I think that would be the most diplomatic way of describing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You decided this was the girl for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Very impressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remain in Fernwald very long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till June '43. From January '46 till-- a month-- a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nah. I was single.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And Rachel was also with you in the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with me. In the same camp, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same camp. That's what I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her parents died here in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was there with her parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you stayed there until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: June '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Palestine. There was no Israel at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wanted to go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Nobody forced me. Nobody forced anybody. When one is young, one is very idealistic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you actually get to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to Haifa. I went by with

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were actually on the

speaker: interviewee
text: On the yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the now. You left Germany? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left Germany. I went to France. To France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel was also amongst the people who were  travelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Rachel and her parents, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With her parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a whole group of people went to France?

speaker: interviewee
text: 4,500 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which part of France did you find yourselves in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not far from Marseilles, I would say. A place-- no, Saint-Chamas. How do you pronounce it? How do you spell it in French? I cannot tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near Marseilles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Marseilles, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was the place that you were going to go to Palestine from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember, the impression of Saint-Chamas going through Marseilles, that was the first time in my life I've seen the sea. I was 23 years old. I've never seen a sea. And suddenly there's so much water. I remember, I was engulfed, you see. I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your breath was taken away--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --by this ocean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remained in Saint-Chamas near Marseilles for a while?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a couple of weeks. It was organized by the Jewish agency.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then off to

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the

speaker: interviewee
text: was a boat that was travelling on the Mississippi.

speaker: interviewer
text: A river boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: A river boat that was equipped for this trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: What? It was specially fitted out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fitted out-- bunks one, two, three, four five, I think it was. You had to slide in, ride like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a-- a wooden boat? A steel ship? What sort of boat--

speaker: interviewee
text: A wooden-- a wooden boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: A wooden boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. A wooden boat. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the conditions were terrible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The conditions were terrible, because there was not much air. But I luckily-- you see, sometimes I have got-- I was resourceful. I-- I don't remember how, but I was on the deck organizing people. Every time people were coming out to having air for-- for a while, and changing. And I was the fellow who was standing outside organizing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the traffic policeman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Traffic policeman. So I was in the air all the time. And sleeping, I was in the hospital under the bed of some sick person, not sleeping in the benches. So [? I had ?] it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how many people were on the

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a huge number.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is  a historical-- now you are coming not to a private story but history. It was a historical event.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a much bigger number of people going to Palestine than usual.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was the largest illegal ship before the establishment of state of Israel that tried to approach in an illegal way-- so-called. Well, the Jews didn't recognize illegal. In a so-called illegal way, enter into Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got onto the It was full of survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Men, women, children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. On the way, as a matter of fact, some pregnant women died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the conditions were so terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because conditions-- I remember being outside, always outside, I remember how they were tossed into the sea.

speaker: interviewer
text: The bodies? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, you-- you had your-- your traffic officer's job on board the The left Marseilles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, on the way to Palestine, to Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: And about a day before arriving to Haifa--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take to get from Marseilles to just a day away from Haifa?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure, but I would say 10 days as a guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: On an awful voyage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was, on a guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't remember suffering from lack of food on No, that was organized by the Jewish agency.

speaker: interviewer
text: And water?

speaker: interviewee
text: And water, yes, yes. The only thing was the amenities were very bad, because you couldn't cater for so many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this huge over-crowding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huge over-crowding, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you're a day out from Haifa.

speaker: interviewee
text: A day out from Haifa, the English. The English were shadowing us all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could see English-- English warships?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know English warships. You could see the color of a warship is different than an ordinary ship. You see, you could see warship?

speaker: interviewer
text: What color is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's grayish, the same you see in Australia. It's always all over the world the same. A warship is different than the other one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could see this warship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [INAUDIBLE] all the time from the very beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Marseilles?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Marseilles, yes. Let's say a few hours after. The-- the intelligence was quite OK. They knew that we were coming out, trying to break the blockade.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're now a day out of Haifa. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Haifa, the English approached us. And the loudspeaker is telling us that we are not permitted to enter Haifa, Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? Give me dates.

speaker: interviewee
text: July. I don't know dates. July definitely. Date I couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year?

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's sometime in July.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Height of summer, '47.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The middle of a hot summer, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So the British said you couldn't go to-- couldn't enter Palestine. What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The British? Well, the-- the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] people who organized the ship were the Haganah. They started throwing-- when they approached, they started throwing on them vegetable. There was no exchange of fire, of course. But they approached us, of course, and then made a dent in the ship, and stopped it, and came in the-- there was a bit of a struggle. A few people were killed, and a number were injured.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the British boarded the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boarded the boat, yes. And took us in tow to Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they towed you into Haifa harbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Haifa, there was not a person to be seen, because the British announced that nobody should get out of their homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a curfew?

speaker: interviewee
text: A curfew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you landed--

speaker: interviewee
text: --landed in Haifa, not seeing one person civil, except the British army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were taken in-- in three ships, 1,500 on each.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were put straight back onto ships?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same day.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't actually spend--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same day. In the same day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the large 4,500 people from the were divided up into three British boats.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, you were just put onto a boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: A boat, it was-- that was not made for people, for passengers. It was just a deep hull, you know, and people were in there. And I somehow again organized myself to be on top. I don't remember how. I don't know. But I-- I was lying on top in the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: You managed to be--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how.

speaker: interviewer
text: --where there was a bit of air.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Rachel and her family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Same ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions like on this ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the ship? Very bad. It was hot. It was July. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No change of clothes. No water-- I mean, for washing, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the British sailors like towards you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you, coming from Russia, you have to realize that everything pales. So I-- personally, I don't see them as very bad. I don't have bad experience. I will tell you why. I had very little knowledge of English. In Russian, you couldn't speak to them. Neither German. Very little knowledge. But a few words I started commanding. And being on top, I approached where there was-- the kitchen was. And I told them, I want to go in there. I don't-- under what pretext-- pretext. And I got back with some [? Nestles, ?] milk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave you a bit of chocolate or condensed milk or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: A bit they gave me, and a bit I took myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't too bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were put on this British ship. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened we were for two months on the sea-- from July till September, when we arrived back in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing? Sailing around the Mediterranean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sailing around the Mediterranean. Because we crossed the Gibraltar, we-- we were in France, where they tried to organize if people want to live-- live in-- that was at that time, it was a struggle between Bevan, the minister of foreign affairs, and Clement Attlee's government, who was very anti-Jewish, very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you had enough food and water on these boats?

speaker: interviewee
text: Water, no problem. Food was a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungry, yes. We got the biscuits. We were-- all biscuits that you could see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the British trying to achieve? I mean, what did they want you to do? Or what did they want to do with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That no more illegal Jewish migrants should go to Palestine. At that time, there was a struggle between the Arabs and the Jews, and the British in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were the British hoping to deposit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, from Germany, all over the world. They succeeded to a degree-- only to a degree.

speaker: interviewer
text: They wanted you anywhere but Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you remained on this British boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Till September.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In September, we came to a camp called Poppendorf. That was not far-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were taken out-- we were-- our boat arrived in Hamburg. And that camp was not far from a city, a well-known city called Lubeck, a German city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you found yourself back--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] in a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this time a camp, it surrounded by military personnel not to be able to get out. A real camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: With barbed wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: With barbed wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop there.
